A **Pressure Regulating Valve (PRV)** at the hospital entrance clamps the pressure to a steady **2 bar**, regardless of upstream chaos. The patient (your MRI machine) never feels the disturbance.
>
> - **UPS = The Hospital's Backup Water Tank:** If the municipal pipe is cut entirely (blackout), the **overhead tank + automatic pump** seamlessly takes over — patients on dialysis never see the interruption. The tank capacity = **battery autonomy (Ah)**; the pump response = **transfer time (ms)**.

### 1.4 Why These Systems Are Critical in Clinical Engineering

| Clinical Hazard | Power Quality Issue | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Ventilator stop** | Blackout (>20 ms) | Patient suffocation |
| **Defibrillator misfire** | Voltage sag / spike | Therapy failure / arrhythmia induction |
| **Image artifact in MRI/CT** | EMI / HF noise on mains | Misdiagnosis |
| **Patient microshock** | Leakage current > 10 µA | Ventricular fibrillation |
| **Infusion pump error** | Frequency drift | Wrong drug dosage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standards (Must Memorize):**
> - **Mains frequency (India):** **50 Hz ± 0.5 Hz**
> - **Single-phase nominal voltage:** **230 V AC RMS**
> - **Three-phase nominal voltage:** **415 V AC RMS** (line-to-line)
> - **Patient leakage current limit (CF type, IEC 60601-1):** **10 µA** (normal) / **50 µA** (single-fault)
> - **Earth leakage current limit:** **≤ 500 µA** (Class I, Type B)
> - **Medical IT system voltage:** **230 V single-phase** with **isolated star-point** (IEC 60364-7-710)
> - **UPS transfer time (online):** **0 ms** | **(line-interactive):** **2–4 ms** | **(offline):** **5–10 ms**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Power Quality Disturbances — Taxonomy

Power line disturbances are classified into **seven categories** per IEEE 1159:

1. **Transients (Impulsive/Oscillatory)** — sub-cycle to few-cycle events (ns to ms), e.g., lightning, capacitor switching.
2. **Short-duration variations** — sags (0.1–0.9 pu, 0.5–30 cycles), swells, interruptions (< 1 min).
3. **Long-duration variations** — sustained under/over-voltages, sustained outages (> 1 min).
4. **Voltage imbalance** — difference in phase voltages (> 2 % in 3-phase).
5. **Waveform distortion** — DC offset, harmonics, interharmonics, notching, noise.
6. **Voltage fluctuations** — flicker (e.g., arc furnaces).
7. **Power frequency variations** — drift of mains frequency beyond tolerance.

### 2.2 Stabilized Power Supply — Operating Principle

#### 2.2.1 Linear Voltage Regulator

A linear regulator uses an **active pass element (transistor)** operated in its linear region to drop excess voltage and dissipate it as heat. Feedback loop compares a sample of $V_{out}$ to a precision reference ($V_{ref}$, typically **2.5 V** from a bandgap cell) and adjusts the pass element's conduction.

**Closed-loop regulation equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

where $R_1$ is the resistor from output to the FB (feedback) pin and $R_2$ is from FB to ground. **Line regulation** quantifies output change per input change:

$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \quad \left[\frac{\mu V}{V}\right]$$

#### 2.2.2 Switch-Mode Power Supply (SMPS)

SMPS uses a high-frequency (typically **50 kHz – 500 kHz**) chopper followed by a transformer/inductor and rectifier. Regulation is achieved by **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** duty-cycle control.

$$V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in} \quad \text{(buck converter)}$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle $\left(0 \leq D \leq 1\right)$. Efficiency is **70 – 95 %** versus linear's **30 – 60 %**.

#### 2.2.3 Ferroresonant Transformer (CVT)

A **Constant Voltage Transformer (CVT)** uses the saturated B-H curve of a special ferroresonant transformer to clamp output voltage. It inherently provides:

- Voltage regulation (± 1 % for input ± 15 %)
- Surge suppression (energy stored in tank LC)
- Galvanic isolation
- No active electronics — highly reliable for **X-ray, CT, MRI auxiliary** supplies.

### 2.3 UPS Topologies — Deep Dive

#### 2.3.1 Offline (Standby / Passive) UPS
- Mains → load directly via transfer switch.
- On mains failure, transfer to inverter (time: **5–10 ms**).
- **No active filtering** of mains noise.

#### 2.3.2 Line-Interactive UPS
- Mains → **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator) buck/boost taps** → load.
- Inverter is **always connected** to DC bus (parallel / bidirectional).
- Compensates sags and swells without using battery.
- Transfer time: **2–4 ms**.

#### 2.3.3 Online (Double-Conversion) UPS
- AC → **Rectifier** → DC bus → **Inverter** → AC load.
- Battery floats on DC bus; takes over with **0 ms transfer**.
- Output is fully regenerated — independent of mains frequency, voltage, or waveform.
- **Required for life-support equipment** (ventilators, anesthesia, ICU monitors, OT).

### 2.4 Isolation Transformer & Medical IT System

A **Medical IT System** uses an **isolating transformer** with a **monitored isolated star-point** and an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)**. The first insulation fault does **not** trip a breaker — it only alarms — preserving surgical OT function.

$$I_{leakage} = \frac{V}{Z_{isolation}} \quad \text{(ideally } \to 0\text{ as } Z_{isolation} \to \infty\text{)}$$

In a medical IT system, the impedance to ground is high (typically > **100 kΩ** at 50 Hz), so first-fault leakage current is clamped to **< 1 mA**, well below the **10 µA** CF-type patient safety threshold **after attenuation**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear regulator output | $V_{out} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Line regulation | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}}$ | µV/V or % |
| 3 | Load regulation | $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 4 | Buck converter output | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ | V; $0 < D < 1$ |
| 5 | Boost converter output | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ | V; $0 < D < 1$ |
| 6 | Buck-boost output | $V_{out} = -V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | V; inverted polarity |
| 7 | UPS autonomy time | $t = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bus}}{P_{load} / \eta_{inv}}$ | Hours (h) |
| 8 | Crest factor | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{RMS}}$ | Dimensionless; ideal sine = **1.414** |
| 9 | THD | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | %; medical UPS **< 5 %** |
| 10 | Power factor | $PF = \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_i^2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Skin depth (copper) | $\delta = \dfrac{66}{\sqrt{f}}$ | mm; for f in Hz |
| 12 | Battery energy | $E = V \cdot C_{Ah}$ | Wh |
| 13 | Inverter efficiency | $\eta_{inv} = \dfrac{P_{out,AC}}{P_{in,DC}} \times 100\%$ | %; typically 90–95 % |
| 14 | Galvanic isolation voltage | $V_{iso} \geq 4000$ V RMS | IEC 60601-1 requirement |
| 15 | Earth leakage (Class I, Type B) | $I_{leak} \leq 500\,\mu A$ | Normal condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Modern hospital-grade UPS systems integrate **double-conversion topology** with **medical IT isolation transformers**, forming the **"IPS (Isolated Power System)"** mandated by **IEC 60364-7-710** for Group 2 medical locations (OT, ICU, cardiac catheterization lab). KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **online vs line-interactive**, the **THD/crest-factor relationship for sensitive imaging loads (MRI, CT, PET)**, and the **autonomy calculation** for a given battery bank.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Buck Converter Output Voltage

**Starting principle:** In steady state, the **volt-second balance** across the inductor must hold — the average voltage across an inductor over one switching period is zero.

During the **ON time** ($t_{on} = D \cdot T_s$), the switch is closed. Voltage across inductor:

$$V_L^{ON} = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

During the **OFF time** ($t_{off} = (1 - D) T_s$), the diode conducts. Voltage across inductor:

$$V_L^{OFF} = -V_{out}$$

**Applying volt-second balance:**

$$V_L^{ON} \cdot t_{on} + V_L^{OFF} \cdot t_{off} = 0$$

Substituting each term:

$$\left(V_{in} - V_{out}\right) \cdot D \cdot T_s + \left(-V_{out}\right) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T_s = 0$$

**Dividing both sides by $T_s$:**

$$\left(V_{in} - V_{out}\right) \cdot D - V_{out} \cdot (1 - D) = 0$$

**Expanding:**

$$V_{in} \cdot D - V_{out} \cdot D - V_{out} + V_{out} \cdot D = 0$$

The $-V_{out} \cdot D$ and $+V_{out} \cdot D$ cancel:

$$V_{in} \cdot D - V_{out} = 0$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The output is a linear fraction of the input, set by the duty cycle. For $D = 0.5$ and $V_{in} = 24$ V, the buck output is **12 V**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: UPS Battery Autonomy

**Given:**
- Load power: $P_{load}$ (Watts)
- Battery voltage: $V_{bus}$ (Volts)
- Battery capacity: $C_{Ah}$ (Ampere-hours)
- Inverter efficiency: $\eta_{inv}$

**Step 1 — DC power demanded from battery:**

$$P_{DC} = \frac{P_{load}}{\eta_{inv}}$$

**Step 2 — DC current drawn from battery:**

$$I_{DC} = \frac{P_{DC}}{V_{bus}} = \frac{P_{load}}{\eta_{inv} \cdot V_{bus}}$$

**Step 3 — Autonomy = capacity ÷ current drawn:**

$$t_{autonomy} = \frac{C_{Ah}}{I_{DC}} = \frac{C_{Ah} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot V_{bus}}{P_{load}}$$

**Equivalent in energy terms:**

$$E_{battery} = V_{bus} \cdot C_{Ah} \quad \text{(Wh)}$$

$$\boxed{t_{autonomy} = \frac{E_{battery} \cdot \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}} \quad \text{(hours)}}$$

> **Sanity check:** For $V_{bus} = 48$ V, $C_{Ah} = 100$ Ah, $\eta_{inv} = 0.92$, $P_{load} = 1000$ W:
>
> $t = \dfrac{48 \times 100 \times 0.92}{1000} = 4.416$ hours ≈ **4 h 25 min**.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Frequently Tested)

> **Problem:** A hospital UPS feeds a **2 kW** load. Battery bank is **48 V / 150 Ah**, inverter efficiency **90 %**. Find (a) autonomy time, (b) minimum UPS rating (kVA) if load PF = 0.85 lagging.

**Part (a):**

$$t = \frac{48 \times 150 \times 0.90}{2000} = \frac{6480}{2000} = 3.24 \text{ hours} = \textbf{3 h 14 min}$$

**Part (b):**

$$S_{UPS} = \frac{P}{PF} = \frac{2000}{0.85} = 2352.94 \text{ VA} \approx \textbf{2.4 kVA}$$

Therefore, a **2.5 kVA** UPS (next standard size) must be selected, with **≥ 25 %** design margin → **3 kVA** rated UPS.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: UPS Topologies

| Parameter | Offline (Standby) | Line-Interactive | Online (Double-Conversion) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transfer time | **5–10 ms** | **2–4 ms** | **0 ms** |
| Output voltage regulation | Poor (±10 %) | Good (±5 %) via AVR | Excellent (±1 %) |
| Surge protection | Basic MOV | Good (AVR + MOV) | Excellent (full isolation) |
| Frequency stability | Mains-locked | Mains-locked | **Inverter-locked (crystal)** |
| THD at output | High (passthrough) | Medium | **< 3 %** |
| Efficiency at full load | 95–98 % | 93–97 % | 88–94 % |
| Heat dissipation | Low | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| Initial cost | Low ₹ | Medium ₹₹ | High ₹₹₹ |
| Clinical use | Non-critical (PC) | Lab equipment | **OT, ICU, life-support** |
| Battery life | 3–5 yrs | 3–5 yrs | 5–8 yrs (with VRLA/ Lithium) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Viva:** "If the equipment has patient contact AND is in a Group 2 location (per IEC 60364-7-710), it MUST be on an **online double-conversion UPS** feeding a **medical IT system**. Anything less is a non-compliance."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Online Double-Conversion UPS with Medical IT Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mains[MAINS 230V AC 50Hz]:::src --> Rec[RECTIFIER + PFC]:::blk
    Rec --> DCbus[DC BUS 48V / 384V]:::bus
    Batt[VRLA / LITHIUM BATTERY]:::batt --> DCbus
    Charger[FLOAT CHARGER]:::blk --> Batt
    DCbus --> Inv[PWM INVERTER IGBT]:::blk
    Inv --> Iso[ISOLATION TRANSFORMER 4kV]:::iso
    Iso --> Filter[EMI/LC OUTPUT FILTER]:::blk
    Filter --> IMD[INSULATION MONITOR IMD]:::blk
    IMD --> OTL[SINGLE-FAULT PANEL]:::out
    OTL --> LoadA[VENTILATOR]:::load
    OTL --> LoadB[ANESTHESIA MACHINE]:::load
    OTL --> LoadC[DEFIBRILLATOR]:::load
    OTL --> LoadD[MONITORING STATION]:::load

    classDef blk fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#084298,color:#000

    classDef out fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000

```

### 4.2 Power Flow — UPS Mode Switching Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MainsNormal
    MainsNormal --> BatteryMode : Mains fails / Sag 20%
    BatteryMode --> MainsNormal : Mains restored + hysteresis
    MainsNormal --> BypassMode : Internal fault / overload
    BypassMode --> MainsNormal : Fault cleared
    BatteryMode --> EndOfDischarge : Vbus < 42V threshold
    EndOfDischarge --> ShutdownAlarm : t = 0 (graceful load shed)
    ShutdownAlarm --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Decision Flow — Which UPS for Which Equipment?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Equipment Selection]:::top --> Q1{Patient Contact?}:::dec
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Offline / Line-Interactive UPS]:::ans
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Group 2 Location? OT / ICU / Cathlab?}:::dec
    Q2 -- No --> A2[Line-Interactive + Isolation Xmer]:::ans
    Q2 -- Yes --> A3[Online UPS + Medical IT System]:::ans
    A3 --> Verify{Verify IEC 60601-1 and IEC 60364-7-710}:::dec
    Verify -- Compliant --> Done[INSTALL]:::top
    Verify -- Non-Compliant --> Reject[REDESIGN]:::ans
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between Online (Double-Conversion) UPS and Line-Interactive UPS in terms of (a) transfer time, (b) output voltage regulation, and (c) typical clinical application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Online UPS | Line-Interactive UPS |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Transfer time | **0 ms** (inverter always feeds load) | **2–4 ms** (AVR + inverter switchover) |
| (b) Output regulation | **±1 %** (regenerated waveform) | **±5 %** (AVR tap switching) |
| (c) Application | **Life-support, OT, ICU, MRI, CT** | Lab instruments, PCs, ultrasound |

*(Full table = 3 marks; any two correct rows = 2 marks; one correct row = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "Medical IT System" as per IEC 60364-7-710. Why is it mandatory in Group 2 medical locations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Medical IT System** is an isolated power distribution network that uses an **isolating transformer with a floating (unearthed) star-point** and an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** to continuously monitor the system's insulation resistance to earth.

**Why mandatory in Group 2 locations (OT, ICU, cardiac catheterization labs):**
1. The **first insulation fault does not trip a protective device** — only an alarm is raised, ensuring **uninterrupted supply** during surgery.
2. It maintains **patient leakage current below 10 µA** (CF-type) under normal conditions, even with a single fault.
3. It conforms to **IEC 60364-7-710** and **IEC 60601-1** safety standards for patient-protected environments.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Topology Analysis + Numerical*

#### **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the working of an **Online Double-Conversion UPS**. Compare its performance with **Offline** and **Line-Interactive** UPS topologies in terms of transfer time, regulation, and clinical suitability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Online Double-Conversion UPS** (also called "true UPS") uses a **two-stage power conversion**:

- **Stage 1:** Mains AC → **Rectifier + PFC** → **DC bus** (battery floats across this bus).
- **Stage 2:** DC bus → **PWM Inverter (IGBT/MOSFET)** → **AC output** → load.

Because the inverter **continuously** feeds the load, there is **zero transfer time** when mains fails. The battery instantly supplies DC current to the bus without any switchover.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn by student):**

```
MAINS → Rectifier → DC Bus (Battery) → Inverter → Output Filter → LOAD
                                       ↑
                                  IMD + Isolation
```

**Comparison Table (5 marks for full table):**

| Parameter | Offline | Line-Interactive | **Online** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transfer time | 5–10 ms | 2–4 ms | **0 ms** |
| Output regulation | ±10 % | ±5 % | **±1 %** |
| Frequency stability | Mains-locked | Mains-locked | **Crystal-locked** |
| THD at output | High | Medium | **< 3 %** |
| Isolation | No | No | **Yes (built-in transformer)** |
| Clinical use | Office PCs | Ultrasound, lab | **OT, ICU, MRI, Ventilators** |

**Conclusion:** Online UPS is the **gold standard** for life-support and patient-contact biomedical equipment.

---

**(b)** A 3 kVA UPS has a battery bank rated **192 V / 100 Ah**. The inverter efficiency is **0.90** and the connected critical load is **2 kW** at PF = 0.85. Calculate: **(i) backup autonomy in hours**, **(ii) the actual UPS load in kW, and (iii) whether the UPS is overloaded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Battery stored energy:**

$$E_{batt} = V_{bus} \times C_{Ah} = 192 \times 100 = 19200 \text{ Wh} = 19.2 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Backup autonomy (i):**

$$t_{autonomy} = \frac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}} = \frac{19200 \times 0.90}{2000}$$

$$\boxed{t_{autonomy} = 8.64 \text{ hours} \approx 8 \text{ h } 38 \text{ min}}$$

**Step 3 — Actual UPS load in kW (ii):**

$$S_{UPS} = 3 \text{ kVA}, \quad PF = 0.85$$

$$P_{UPS} = S \times PF = 3 \times 0.85 = 2.55 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Overload check (iii):**

Connected load = **2 kW**; UPS real power capacity = **2.55 kW**.

$$\text{Loading} = \frac{2.0}{2.55} \times 100\% = 78.4\%$$

Since loading is **< 100 %**, the UPS is **not overloaded**, with a **21.6 %** safety margin. **(3 marks)**

**Final Answer:** Autonomy = **8.64 h**, Real capacity = **2.55 kW**, Status = **Safe**.

---

#### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Stabilizers + Medical IT*

#### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Ferroresonant Constant Voltage Transformer (CVT)** as a stabilized power supply. List four advantages for hospital use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **CVT (Constant Voltage Transformer)** is a special transformer whose secondary is **deliberately saturated** by design. Its operation relies on the **non-linear B-H curve** of the core.

**Construction:**
- Primary winding on a normal core limb.
- Secondary winding on a **saturated limb** (smaller cross-section).
- A **resonant tank capacitor** is connected across the secondary, forming an **LC tank circuit** tuned near the 3rd harmonic (150 Hz) of the 50 Hz supply.

**Working:**
1. When input voltage **rises**, the secondary flux is already saturated → no proportional increase in secondary voltage → output stays constant.
2. When input voltage **falls**, the saturated core still operates in the flat region of B-H → secondary voltage remains steady.
3. The **LC tank** stores energy and suppresses incoming **transients/surges** (up to 2 kV).
4. Output regulation: **±1 %** for input variation of **±15 %**.

**Advantages for Hospital Use (any four):**

1. **No active electronics** → extremely reliable, MTBF > **100,000 hours**.
2. **Built-in surge suppression** — protects X-ray tubes and CT gantries.
3. **Galvanic isolation** — reduces patient leakage risk.
4. **Resistant to input waveform distortion** — works with distorted mains.
5. **Self-protected** against output short-circuit (current limiting inherent).
6. **Maintenance-free** — no batteries to replace (unlike UPS).

---

**(b)** Describe a **Medical IT System** with block diagram. Explain the role of the **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** and state two consequences of the first insulation fault. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
MAINS → Medical Isolation Transformer (1:1, 230V) → Distribution Panel
                                                         ↓
                                          IMD (Insulation Monitor)
                                                         ↓
                                              OT/ICU Loads (ventilator, etc.)
                                              Equipotential bonding bus
```

**Components & Roles:**

1. **Isolation Transformer:** 1:1 ratio, **5 kVA – 10 kVA** typical, with reinforced isolation (≥ 4 kV) between primary and secondary. Star-point is **unearthed (floating)**.

2. **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD):** Continuously measures insulation resistance between the IT system's live conductors and earth using a **DC measuring voltage** or **low-frequency AC**. Generates an **audible + visual alarm** when insulation resistance drops below **50 kΩ**.

3. **Line-to-line and line-to-earth voltmeters:** Monitor phase voltages and first-fault voltage to earth.

**Consequences of First Insulation Fault:**

1. **No tripping** — the protective device (circuit breaker) does **not** operate. Power supply to OT/ICU is **maintained**, allowing surgery/therapy to complete safely.
2. **Alarm is raised** — clinical/biomedical staff are notified to arrange repair after the procedure. The faulty circuit can be located using an **Insulation Fault Locator (IFL)**.
3. **Leakage current remains limited** because the impedance to earth is high (> 100 kΩ) → patient microshock risk is contained.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Evaluators' Report):**
> 1. **Confusing "transfer time" of online UPS as "instantaneous."** Correct answer: **0 ms** (NOT "1 ms" or "very fast"). Half mark lost per wrong unit.
> 2. **Forgetting efficiency** in the autonomy formula: $t = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bus} \cdot \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}}$ — many students omit $\eta_{inv}$. Lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing kVA instead of kW** (or vice-versa) when PF is given. Lose **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 4. **Medical IT ≠ UPS.** They serve **different** purposes: UPS = backup power; Medical IT = isolation + continuity. Examiners will deduct if confused.
> 5. **Skipping the IMD explanation** in Medical IT — it carries **2 marks** standalone.
> 6. **Not stating the standard** (IEC 60601-1 / IEC 60364-7-710) — bonus awareness, often **+0.5 mark** if mentioned.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Stabilized Supply** = constant $V_{out}$ against input/load/temperature variation. Two main families: **Linear regulators** (low noise, inefficient) and **SMPS** (high efficiency, noisy without filtering).
- ✅ **UPS** = battery-backed **seamless** power. Three topologies: **Offline**, **Line-Interactive**, **Online**.
- ✅ **Online UPS** has **0 ms** transfer → mandatory for OT/ICU/life-support.
- ✅ **Buck converter equation:** $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$; **Boost:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$.
- ✅ **Autonomy formula:** $t = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bus} \cdot \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}}$ **hours**.
- ✅ **Medical IT System** uses an isolating transformer with **floating star-point** + **IMD**. First fault → alarm only (no trip).
- ✅ **Key Indian standards:** IEC 60601-1, IEC 60364-7-710, IEEE 1159 (power quality), IS 14695 (UPS).
- ✅ **Patient leakage (CF):** **10 µA** (normal), **50 µA** (single-fault).
- ✅ **Battery technologies:** **VRLA** (5–8 yr), **Lithium-ion** (10 yr), **NiCd** (15 yr, ICU preferred).
- ✅ **Crest factor** for medical loads: ≥ **3:1** (CT scanner demands this from UPS).
- ✅ **THD** for medical-grade UPS output: **< 5 %** (IEC 62040-3).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protective Systems: Over Voltage and Over Current Protectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT413 - CLINICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Protective Systems: Over Voltage and Over Current Protectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Over Voltage Protection (OVP)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Over Voltage Protection (OVP)** is a class of power supply and electronic circuit protection engineering designed to safeguard sensitive medical instrumentation against voltage transients, surges, and sustained over-voltage conditions that exceed the nominal operating voltage rating of the equipment. The standard reference is **IEC 60601-1** (Medical Electrical Equipment Safety) and **IEC 61000-4-5** (Surge Immunity).

In clinical environments, the safe touch voltage limit for medical instruments is **25 V AC** or **60 V DC** under normal conditions (Type CF cardiac-protected equipment), making OVP a critical patient-safety subsystem.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Relief Valve"

Imagine a domestic water tap connected to a municipal pipeline. If the municipality accidentally pumps water at **10× the normal pressure**, your tap, pipes, and geyser will burst. A **Pressure Relief Valve** is installed on the boiler — it does *not* stop the over-pressure from occurring, but it **diverts or clamps** the excess energy to ground before it can rupture the system.

In the same way:
- The **mains supply** = the high-pressure pipeline
- The **medical device (ECG, ventilator, defibrillator)** = the boiler
- The **OVP circuit** = the pressure relief valve that shunts excess voltage safely to earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The OVP subsystem in a medical device is *not* optional — it is mandated by **IEC 60601-1, Clause 8.5** (Protection against Over-Voltage Conditions). Examiners expect students to know that OVP falls under the "Means of Patient Protection (MOPP)" framework.

---

## 1.2 Over Current Protection (OCP)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Over Current Protection (OCP)** is a protective engineering discipline involving components and circuits that automatically interrupt or limit electrical current when it exceeds a predetermined safe threshold, thereby preventing conductor overheating, insulation breakdown, fire hazards, and patient burns in clinical electromedical equipment.

The governing standards include **IEC 60601-1 (Clauses 8.4 and 13)**, **IEC 60950-1**, and **IS 8828** for low-voltage switchgear.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Main Switch (MCB)"

Think of the **Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB)** in your home distribution board. When you accidentally overload a single socket with too many appliances drawing current simultaneously, the MCB *trips* and disconnects the circuit within milliseconds. You cannot draw infinite current because the MCB enforces a contractual "maximum withdrawal limit" — similar to a bank ATM capping your daily withdrawal.

In medical electronics:
- The **branch circuit** = the wall socket powering the patient monitor
- The **MCB / Fuse** = the ATM-style withdrawal cap
- The **patient/operator** = the protected entity downstream

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** OVP protects against **voltage anomalies**, while OCP protects against **current anomalies**. In a medical device, both layers must operate **independently and redundantly** — a principle called **Defence-in-Depth** in biomedical safety engineering.

---

## 1.3 Classification Matrix of Protective Devices

| Hazard Type | Transient (µs–ms) | Sustained (s–min) |
|---|---|---|
| **Over-Voltage** | TVS Diode, MOV, GDT, Spark Gap | Crowbar SCR, Shunt Regulator, OVP IC |
| **Over-Current** | PTC Resettable, HRC Fuse | MCB, MCCB, Thermal/Bimetallic Breaker |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Transient protectors react in **microseconds to nanoseconds**; sustained protectors react in **milliseconds to seconds**. The KTU exam frequently tests this **response-time taxonomy**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Over-Voltage Protector — Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)

A **Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)** is a non-linear resistor fabricated from sintered zinc oxide (ZnO) grains with bismuth and other oxide dopants. Its **V–I characteristic** is highly non-linear:

$$I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$$

where **α** (alpha) typically lies between **25 and 50** for a high-quality MOV, and **k** is a device constant. The steep exponent explains why the MOV behaves like an open circuit at normal voltages and a near-short circuit at surge voltages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why α matters:** A higher α gives a sharper "knee" in the V–I curve, meaning the MOV clamps more abruptly. For medical-grade MOVs, α ≥ 35 is recommended.

**Clamping Voltage Equation** (Energy Balance Method):
$$V_c = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{I_p}{I_{ref}}\right)^{1/\alpha}$$

where:
- $V_c$ = Clamping voltage at peak surge current $I_p$
- $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage at test current $I_{ref}$ (usually 1 mA)
- $I_p$ = Peak surge current (8/20 µs waveform)

### 2.1.2 Transient Voltage Suppressor (TVS) Diode

A **TVS diode** is a specialised avalanche breakdown diode that operates in the **avalanche breakdown region** of its reverse-biased characteristic. Unlike a standard Zener, the TVS is rated for **high peak pulse power** (400 W to 15 kW for 10/1000 µs pulses).

**Clamping Factor**:
$$\eta_{clamp} = \frac{V_{clamp}}{V_{RWM}}$$

where $V_{RWM}$ is the **Reverse Working Maximum** (stand-off) voltage. For an ideal protector, $\eta_{clamp} \rightarrow 1$.

### 2.1.3 Gas Discharge Tube (GDT)

A **GDT** is a sealed ceramic chamber filled with inert gas (argon, neon) at low pressure. When the voltage across the electrodes exceeds the **DC spark-over voltage** $V_s$, the gas ionises and forms a low-impedance arc ($R_{arc} \approx 0.1$ to 1 Ω). Once the fault current drops below the **extinction current** $I_e$ (typically 10–50 mA), the arc quenches and the GDT resets automatically.

### 2.1.4 Crowbar Protection Circuit

A **crowbar** uses an **SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier)** placed across the DC supply. When the OVP detector IC senses an over-voltage, it fires the SCR gate, which **short-circuits the supply** to ground, blowing the upstream fuse. This is a "one-shot" sacrificial protection — common in **defibrillator capacitor banks** and **X-ray generator high-voltage sections**.

---

## 2.2 Over-Current Protector — Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Fuse — The Sacrificial Link

A **fuse** is the simplest OCP device — a calibrated metal wire/film that melts when $I^2 t$ (let-through energy) exceeds the **melting $I^2 t$ rating**.

**Pre-Arcing $I^2 t$** (energy required to melt the element):
$$\int_{0}^{t_m} i^2(t) \, dt = K$$

For a sinusoidal current of RMS value $I_{rms}$ and melting time $t_m$:
$$I_{rms}^2 \cdot t_m = \text{const}$$

This is the **fuse time-current characteristic**: doubling the current reduces the melting time to one-quarter.

### 2.2.2 Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB)

An **MCB** uses a **bimetallic strip** (thermal trip for sustained overloads) and a **solenoid** (magnetic trip for short circuits). The trip curve is classified as **B (3–5 × In)**, **C (5–10 × In)**, or **D (10–20 × In)**.

### 2.2.3 PTC Resettable Fuse (PolySwitch)

A **PTC** is a conductive polymer whose resistance increases by 3–4 orders of magnitude when the temperature exceeds the **Curie switching temperature** $T_s$. Self-heating due to $I^2 R$ loss triggers the transition. After fault removal and cooling, the device resets automatically.

**Resistance Ratio**:
$$\frac{R_{trip}}{R_{initial}} \geq 10^3$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MOV V–I law | $I = k \cdot V^{\alpha}$ | A, V | $\alpha \in [25, 50]$ |
| 2 | MOV Clamping | $V_c = V_{ref}(I_p/I_{ref})^{1/\alpha}$ | V | $I_p \leq I_{max}$ |
| 3 | TVS Clamp Factor | $\eta_{clamp} = V_{clamp}/V_{RWM}$ | — | $\eta_{clamp} \geq 1$ |
| 4 | Fuse Melting Energy | $I_{rms}^2 \cdot t_m = K$ | A²s | Constant for given fuse |
| 5 | Power Dissipation | $P_d = I^2 \cdot R$ or $P_d = V^2/R$ | W | Steady-state |
| 6 | PTC Trip Time | $t_{trip} = \frac{\tau \cdot \ln(\Delta T / \Delta T_0)}{I^2 R - P_{diss}}$ | s | Self-heating model |
| 7 | Let-Through Energy | $E = \int i^2 \, dt$ | A²s | Bounded by fuse $I^2t$ |
| 8 | Surge Power | $P_{peak} = V_{clamp} \cdot I_{peak}$ | W | 8/20 µs waveform |
| 9 | Energy Absorbed (MOV) | $W = V_c \cdot I_p \cdot t_{pulse}$ | J | For rectangular pulse |
| 10 | Earth Leakage Trip | $I_{\Delta n} \leq 30 \text{ mA (medical)}$ | A | IEC 60601-1 patient aux |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **standard reference voltage** ($V_{ref} = 1$ mA for MOVs) and the **surge waveform** (8/20 µs or 10/1000 µs) when solving OVP numericals. Marks are awarded for these contextual statements.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Patient Monitoring (ECG, SpO₂, EEG):** MOV + TVS combo on input power port and signal lines (per IEC 60601-1-2 EMC).
- **Defibrillators:** Crowbar OVP on the high-voltage capacitor bank (up to 360 J stored energy).
- **MRI Gradient Amplifiers:** OCP fuses rated for **high DC voltages** (up to 2000 V) with $I^2t$ coordination.
- **X-Ray Generators:** GDTs on the high-tension transformer primary for lightning-induced surges.
- **Infusion Pumps / Dialysis Machines:** PTC resettable fuses on battery charging circuits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MOV Clamping Voltage Under Surge

**Problem Setup:**
A medical-grade MOV has a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 275$ V at $I_{ref} = 1$ mA, and a non-linearity exponent $\alpha = 35$. A lightning-induced surge of peak current $I_p = 1000$ A is applied (8/20 µs waveform). Determine the clamping voltage $V_c$.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation:**

$$V_c = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{I_p}{I_{ref}}\right)^{1/\alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\frac{I_p}{I_{ref}} = \frac{1000 \text{ A}}{0.001 \text{ A}} = 10^6$$

$$V_c = 275 \cdot (10^6)^{1/35}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent:**

$$\frac{1}{35} = 0.02857$$

$$(10^6)^{0.02857} = 10^{6 \times 0.02857} = 10^{0.1714}$$

**Step 4 — Convert logarithmic value to numeric:**

$$10^{0.1714} = e^{0.1714 \cdot \ln 10} = e^{0.1714 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.3947}$$

$$e^{0.3947} \approx 1.484$$

**Step 5 — Compute final clamping voltage:**

$$V_c = 275 \times 1.484 \approx 408.1 \text{ V}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1000 A surge is clamped to ~408 V — well below the 1500 V breakdown of a typical MOV but safely above the 230 V nominal mains. The protected downstream medical device sees only 408 V, avoiding insulation failure.

> [!NOTE]
> The **decoupling ratio** $V_c / V_{nominal} = 408/230 = 1.77$ is called the **clamping ratio**. A lower ratio is better, achieved by selecting MOVs with higher α.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fuse Melting Time Under Sinusoidal Overload

**Problem Setup:**
A HRC (High Rupturing Capacity) fuse has a melting $I^2t$ constant of $K = 12000$ A²s. It carries a sinusoidal overload current of $I_{rms} = 100$ A. Determine the melting time $t_m$.

**Step 1 — Recall the time-current characteristic:**

$$I_{rms}^2 \cdot t_m = K$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $t_m$:**

$$t_m = \frac{K}{I_{rms}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$t_m = \frac{12000}{(100)^2} = \frac{12000}{10000} = 1.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Verification with double-current test:**

If the current doubles to 200 A:

$$t_m' = \frac{12000}{(200)^2} = \frac{12000}{40000} = 0.3 \text{ s}$$

Ratio check: $t_m' / t_m = 0.3 / 1.2 = 0.25$ → confirms inverse-square law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This inverse-square behaviour is the **physical basis of fuse discrimination** in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Peak Surge Power Dissipation in TVS

**Problem Setup:**
A bi-directional TVS diode has $V_{RWM} = 24$ V and $V_{clamp} = 32$ V at $I_{PP} = 50$ A (10/1000 µs pulse). Calculate the peak surge power and the energy absorbed.

**Step 1 — Peak surge power:**

$$P_{PP} = V_{clamp} \cdot I_{PP} = 32 \times 50 = 1600 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed (rectangular pulse approximation):**

$$W = P_{PP} \cdot t_{pulse} = 1600 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — For a more accurate exponential waveform** ($i(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau}$):

$$W = V_{clamp} \cdot I_0 \cdot \tau \cdot (1 - e^{-t_p/\tau})$$

For $t_p \gg \tau$, $W \approx V_{clamp} \cdot I_0 \cdot \tau$.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU numericals typically use the **rectangular approximation** for simplicity. Always mention this assumption to gain full method marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | MOV | TVS Diode | GDT | Crowbar (SCR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Response Time | 1–25 ns | < 1 ps | 50–500 ns | 1–10 µs |
| Clamping Voltage | Moderate (1.7–2× V_nom) | Tight (1.3–1.5× V_RWM) | Very Low (arc drop) | Near zero |
| Leakage Current | µA | µA | None (open) | None (open) |
| Reset | Automatic | Sacrificial | Automatic | Replace fuse |
| Peak Current | 6.5 kA (typ) | 0.5–15 kW | 20–100 kA | 100–1000 A |
| Use in Medical PSU | Mains input | DC rails | Outdoor/lab mains | HV capacitor bank |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** "*MOV is slow but tough, TVS is fast but small, GDT is huge but slow, Crowbar is sacrificial but absolute.*"

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Medical Device Protection System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mains["MAINS INPUT 230V 50Hz"] --> SPD1["Stage1 GDT Class I"]
    SPD1 --> SPD2["Stage2 MOV Class II"]
    SPD2 --> SPD3["Stage3 TVS Stage III"]
    SPD3 --> ISO["Medical Isolation Transformer IEC 60601 1"]
    ISO --> RECT["Bridge Rectifier and SMPS"]
    RECT --> FUSE["HRC Fuse OC Protection"]
    RECT --> PTC["PTC Resettable Secondary Protection"]
    RECT --> CROWBAR["Crowbar SCR OVP for HV rail"]
    FUSE --> DCBUS["DC Bus 24V 48V"]
    PTC --> DCBUS
    CROWBAR --> DCBUS
    DCBUS --> MON["Patient Monitor ECG SpO2"]
    DCBUS --> PUMP["Infusion Pump Driver"]
    DCBUS --> DEFIB["Defibrillator HV Bank"]
    DCBUS --> LEAK["RCD 30mA Patient Earth Leakage"]
    LEAK --> EARTH["Protective Earth Conductor"]

    classDef input fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000;
    classDef protection fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f59f00,color:#000;
    classDef power fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000;
    classDef load fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000;

    class Mains input;
    class SPD1,SPD2,SPD3,FUSE,PTC,CROWBAR,LEAK protection;
    class ISO,RECT,DCBUS power;
    class MON,PUMP,DEFIB load;
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **3-stage cascaded protection architecture** follows **IEC 61643-1 (SPD standards)**: Stage I (GDT) absorbs lightning-class energy, Stage II (MOV) handles switching transients, Stage III (TVS) clamps residual spikes to ASIC-tolerable levels.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Selecting an Over-Voltage Protector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start Selection"] --> Q1{"Is the fault energy high kJ?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> GDT["Choose GDT Class I"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is the response time critical ns?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> TVS["Choose TVS Diode"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is leakage current a concern?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> GDT2["Choose GDT or Crowbar"]
    Q3 -- No --> MOV["Choose MOV Class II"]
    MOV --> END["Validate with IEC 60601 1"]
    TVS --> END
    GDT --> END
    GDT2 --> END
```

---

## 4.3 Time-Current Characteristic of an OCP Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Long Time Overload Region minutes"] --> B["Thermal Trip Bimetallic"]
    B --> C["Short Time Region seconds"]
    C --> D["Magnetic Trip Solenoid"]
    D --> E["Instantaneous Region 10ms"]
    E --> F["High Rupturing Fuse Clearing"]
    F --> G["Current Axis Log scale In"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram corresponds to the **standard inverse-time characteristic** of an MCB + Fuse coordination, where the slowest device trips first and the fastest device backs it up (a KTU favourite for short-answer questions).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Over Voltage Protection (OVP)**. List any two over-voltage protection devices used in medical equipment with their working principles.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Over Voltage Protection (OVP) refers to the use of protective components that clamp, shunt, or disconnect voltage transients exceeding the safe operating rating of a medical device, ensuring patient and operator safety as mandated by **IEC 60601-1, Clause 8.5**.

**Device 1 — MOV (1 mark):**
A **Metal Oxide Varistor (MOV)** is a non-linear ZnO-based resistor with V–I relation $I = kV^{\alpha}$, where $\alpha$ lies between 25–50. At normal mains voltage it presents high impedance; during a surge, it becomes a low-impedance shunt to earth, clamping the voltage to a safe level.

**Device 2 — TVS Diode (1 mark):**
A **Transient Voltage Suppressor (TVS)** is an avalanche-mode diode that conducts during over-voltage events within picoseconds, clamping the line to within 1.3–1.5× its reverse working voltage $V_{RWM}$. It is preferred for low-energy, high-speed protection of DC rails in patient monitors.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Explain the working of a **PTC resettable fuse**. State one advantage and one disadvantage compared to a conventional HRC fuse.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Working (1.5 marks):**
A **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) resettable fuse** is made of a conductive polymer whose resistivity rises sharply above the **Curie switching temperature** $T_s$. During an over-current, $I^2R$ self-heating pushes the polymer past $T_s$, causing the resistance to jump by 3–4 orders of magnitude, thereby limiting the current. Once the fault clears and the device cools, it automatically resets to its low-resistance state.

**Advantage (0.75 mark):** Resettable — no replacement needed after a transient fault, reducing maintenance downtime in critical care equipment.

**Disadvantage (0.75 mark):** Higher initial resistance (typically 0.1–1 Ω) causes continuous power loss, and slower trip time (seconds) compared to a sub-millisecond HRC fuse.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** question from the choice. Each question has sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Total = 14 marks.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks): **Over-Voltage Protection in Medical Power Supplies**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **three-stage cascaded SPD architecture** used in a medical-grade power supply unit, clearly stating the role of GDT, MOV, and TVS in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A medical-grade PSU must withstand both **lightning-induced surges** (up to 10 kA, 8/20 µs) and **switching transients** (up to 2 kV, 1.2/50 µs) on the AC mains, while delivering clean isolated power to patient-connected circuits. The IEC 61643-1 **cascaded SPD architecture** is used:

**Stage I — Class I SPD (GDT) [2 marks]:**
A **Gas Discharge Tube** is placed across L–N and L–E. It has very high surge-handling capacity (20–100 kA) but slow response (µs). It conducts the bulk of lightning energy to earth. Its high follow-current is limited by the upstream impedance.

**Stage II — Class II SPD (MOV) [2 marks]:**
A **Metal Oxide Varistor** clamps residual switching transients to ~1.7–2× the nominal mains voltage. It has fast response (ns) and moderate energy absorption (typically 200–1000 J). It works in coordination with the GDT — handling the medium-energy, fast-rising portion of the surge.

**Stage III — Class III SPD (TVS) [2 marks]:**
A **TVS diode** at the SMPS input clamps any residual spike to below 50 V, protecting the rectifier and DC-DC converter. Response is sub-nanosecond; it handles the small-energy, very-fast residual transients.

**Decoupling Impedance (1 mark):**
Between stages, an **inductance (10–20 µH)** or **length of cable** provides decoupling impedance that forces each stage to share the surge energy according to its rating.

> A neat block diagram should be drawn: `Mains → L(N) ── GDT ── Inductor ── MOV ── Inductor ── TVS → SMPS → Isolated DC Rails`. *(1 mark for diagram)*

---

**(b)** A MOV used in the input stage of a medical device has reference voltage $V_{ref} = 320$ V at $I_{ref} = 1$ mA and non-linearity exponent $\alpha = 30$. Calculate the clamping voltage when a peak surge current of $I_p = 500$ A (8/20 µs waveform) passes through it. Comment on the safety margin with respect to the 230 V mains. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (0.5 mark):**
- $V_{ref} = 320$ V
- $I_{ref} = 1$ mA $= 0.001$ A
- $\alpha = 30$
- $I_p = 500$ A

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$V_c = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{I_p}{I_{ref}}\right)^{1/\alpha}$$

**Step 1 — Current ratio (0.5 mark):**

$$\frac{I_p}{I_{ref}} = \frac{500}{0.001} = 5 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2 — Exponent evaluation (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.0333$$

$$(5 \times 10^5)^{0.0333} = 10^{0.0333 \times \log_{10}(5 \times 10^5)}$$

$$\log_{10}(5 \times 10^5) = \log_{10} 5 + 5 = 0.6990 + 5 = 5.6990$$

$$0.0333 \times 5.6990 = 0.1898$$

$$10^{0.1898} = e^{0.1898 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.4370} \approx 1.548$$

**Step 3 — Clamping voltage (1 mark):**

$$V_c = 320 \times 1.548 \approx 495.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Safety margin analysis (2.5 marks):**

- Nominal mains (RMS) = 230 V → Peak = $230\sqrt{2} = 325.3$ V
- Clamping ratio = $\frac{495.4}{230} \approx 2.15$
- This is **above** the recommended 1.7–2.0× ratio for medical-grade MOVs.
- **Conclusion:** The MOV with $\alpha = 30$ provides insufficient clamping for medical safety. Recommendation: select a MOV with $\alpha \geq 40$ and $V_{ref} = 275$ V, which would yield a lower $V_c \approx 380$ V and clamping ratio ≈ 1.65 — safer for ECG/EEG front-ends.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks): **Over-Current Protection Coordination**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **operating principle of an MCB** with reference to its thermal and magnetic trip units. Compare its characteristics with that of a **HRC fuse**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MCB Principle (3.5 marks):**
A **Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB)** is a re-settable OCP device containing two trip mechanisms in series:

1. **Thermal Trip (Bimetallic Strip):** A strip of two metals with different thermal expansion coefficients (e.g., Invar and brass) bends when heated by sustained overload current (1.05–1.3 × In). The bending force trips a mechanical latch, opening the contacts. Trip time: **seconds to minutes**, suitable for sustained overloads.

2. **Magnetic Trip (Solenoid/Plunger):** A coil carrying the line current produces a magnetic field proportional to current. When the current exceeds 3–5× In (B-curve), 5–10× In (C-curve), or 10–20× In (D-curve), the magnetic force drives a plunger that instantaneously opens the contacts. Trip time: **< 10 ms**, suitable for short-circuit currents.

**HRC Fuse Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | MCB | HRC Fuse |
|---|---|---|
| Reusability | Re-settable manually | Sacrificial (replace) |
| Trip Time (SC) | 10–100 ms | 0.5–5 ms |
| Energy Let-Through | Higher | Lower (current-limiting) |
| Breaking Capacity | 6–25 kA | 50–120 kA |
| Coordination | Selective with upstream | Needs back-up MCB |
| Medical Use | Branch circuits, sub-distribution | Main isolation, HV circuits |

**Conclusion:** MCBs offer operational convenience in medical wards (e.g., ICU branch circuits), while HRC fuses protect higher-energy systems such as MRI gradient amplifiers and X-ray primary feeders.

---

**(b)** A HRC fuse has a pre-arcing $I^2t$ rating of $K = 8000$ A²s. It carries a fault current of $I_{sc} = 400$ A. Calculate: (i) the pre-arcing melting time, and (ii) the peak let-through energy if the arcing time is $t_{arc} = 5$ ms. Comment on the suitability of this fuse for a 230 V medical isolation circuit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (0.5 mark):**
- $I^2t = K = 8000$ A²s
- $I_{sc} = 400$ A
- $t_{arc} = 5$ ms $= 0.005$ s

**Part (i) — Melting time (2 marks):**

$$t_m = \frac{K}{I_{sc}^2} = \frac{8000}{(400)^2} = \frac{8000}{160000} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

**Part (ii) — Peak let-through energy (2 marks):**

For sinusoidal current, RMS value is $I_{rms} = 400$ A, and assuming the fuse clears near the next current zero, the total clearing time is:

$$t_{clear} = t_m + t_{arc} = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ ms}$$

Total $I^2t$ let-through:

$$I^2t_{total} = I_{sc}^2 \cdot t_{clear} = (400)^2 \times 0.055 = 160000 \times 0.055 = 8800 \text{ A²s}$$

**Part (iii) — Suitability for medical isolation (2.5 marks):**

- For a 230 V medical isolation circuit (per IEC 60601-1), the prospective short-circuit current at the secondary is typically limited to **< 1000 A** by the isolation transformer's impedance.
- A 400 A fault is therefore realistic, and the 50 ms melting time is acceptable — it allows downstream coordination with circuit breakers in patient-connected subcircuits.
- The total let-through energy of 8800 A²s is within the **withstand rating** of typical medical-grade cables (e.g., $I^2t_{cable} \approx 25000$ A²s for 2.5 mm² Cu).
- **Conclusion:** The fuse is suitable, but **discrimination** with downstream MCBs must be verified using the time-current coordination curves.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing 2–4 marks per question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mA → A** when computing $I_p / I_{ref}$ in MOV numericals. This single error cascades and gives the wrong $V_c$. Always write the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{ref}$ with $V_{clamp}$**: $V_{ref}$ is at 1 mA DC test, $V_{clamp}$ is at surge current — they are **not equal**. Students often skip this distinction.
> 3. **Using peak current instead of RMS** in fuse $I^2t$ calculations. The $I^2t$ law uses **RMS** unless otherwise specified.
> 4. **Omitting the standard reference** (IEC 60601-1, IEC 61000-4-5). KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for citing the correct standard.
> 5. **Drawing a one-line block diagram** for cascaded SPD. You must show **three distinct stages** with decoupling impedance, not a single block.
> 6. **In PTC questions, omitting the $R_{trip}/R_{initial} \geq 10^3$ ratio** — this is the most-missed KTU sub-question.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OVP vs OCP:** Voltage anomaly protection vs current anomaly protection — both essential in IEC 60601-1 compliant medical devices.
- **3-Stage SPD Cascade:** GDT (slow, high-energy) → MOV (medium, medium-energy) → TVS (fast, low-energy) — coordinated by inter-stage inductance.
- **MOV Equation:** $I = kV^{\alpha}$, with $\alpha \in [25, 50]$. Higher α = sharper clamping.
- **TVS:** Sub-ns response, used on DC rails; clamping factor $\eta_{clamp} = V_{clamp}/V_{RWM}$.
- **GDT:** Sealed gas tube; high surge current, slow; extinction current $I_e$ must be exceeded to reset.
- **Crowbar (SCR):** One-shot protection for HV capacitor banks (defibrillators, X-ray); blows upstream fuse.
- **Fuse Time-Current Law:** $I_{rms}^2 \cdot t_m = K$ → inverse-square relationship.
- **MCB Trip Curves:** B (3–5× In), C (5–10× In), D (10–20× In).
- **PTC Resettable:** Resistance ratio ≥ 10³, auto-resetting, used in patient-connected secondary protection.
- **Standards to Memorise:** IEC 60601-1 (medical safety), IEC 61000-4-5 (surge immunity), IEC 61643-1 (SPD coordination).
- **Medical Earth Leakage:** $I_{\Delta n} \leq 30$ mA for patient auxiliary circuits; 10 mA for cardiac (Type CF).
- **Key Conversion:** 1 mA = $10^{-3}$ A; $8/20$ µs = standard lightning waveform; $10/1000$ µs = standard TVS pulse.
- **Coordination Principle:** Slowest downstream device trips first; upstream fuse is the final back-up.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Protectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT413 - CLINICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circuit Protectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circuit Protectors** are electrical safety devices designed to automatically detect abnormal current, voltage, or fault conditions in a circuit and interrupt the power supply to prevent damage to equipment, fire hazards, and — most critically in clinical engineering — protect patients and clinical staff from electric shock, burns, and macroshock/microshock injuries.

In the context of **Clinical Engineering**, circuit protectors form the **first line of defense** in the hospital electrical safety chain. They are mandated by standards such as **IEC 60601-1** (Medical Electrical Equipment Safety), **NFPA 99** (Health Care Facilities Code), and **IS 732** (Indian Wiring Regulations).

The principal circuit protectors encountered in biomedical/hospital installations are:

1. **Fuses** (one-time thermal/overcurrent protectors)
2. **Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCBs)** (resettable thermal-magnetic protectors)
3. **Molded Case Circuit Breakers (MCCBs)** (for higher ratings)
4. **Earth Leakage Circuit Breakers (ELCBs) / Residual Current Devices (RCDs / RCCBs)**
5. **Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters (GFCIs)** — the North American equivalent of RCCBs
6. **Isolation Transformers (Line Isolation Monitors — LIMs)**
7. **Surge Protective Devices (SPDs)**
8. **Overload Relays** and **Thermal Cutouts**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Body's Immune System:** Think of circuit protectors as the **immune system of a hospital's electrical network**. Just as white blood cells rush to neutralize an infection (fault) before it spreads through the body (the hospital), a fuse "sacrifices itself" (blows) or a circuit breaker "trips" to disconnect a faulty section before damage spreads. In a clinical environment, the "infection" could be a leakage current as small as **10 µA** passing through a patient's heart (microshock), which is 1000× more sensitive than a healthy adult's perception threshold.

### Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Let-through energy (I²t):** Energy allowed through a protective device before disconnection — measured in **A²·s**.
- **Breaking Capacity (Icn):** Maximum fault current the device can safely interrupt — typically **6 kA, 10 kA, 16 kA, 25 kA** for MCBs.
- **Trip Curve:** B (3–5 In), C (5–10 In), D (10–20 In) — where **In** is the rated current.
- **RCD Sensitivity:** **30 mA** (personal protection), **300 mA** (fire protection), **6 mA** (medical/patient protection per IEC 60601).
- **MIC (Maximum Allowable Current):** Limit below which ventricular fibrillation risk is negligible — **10 mA (AC)** for macroshock, **10 µA** directly applied to the heart for microshock.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Fuse

A fuse is the **simplest, cheapest, and most reliable** overcurrent protector. It contains a calibrated metallic element (usually **zinc, silver, or copper-tin alloy**) that melts when the heat generated by excessive current ($I^2R$ heating) exceeds its thermal capacity.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. **Normal current** flows → I²R heating is below the melting threshold → element remains intact.
2. **Overload or short circuit** occurs → I²R heating rises sharply.
3. The element reaches its **melting point** (e.g., **960 °C** for silver, **419 °C** for zinc).
4. Element melts → opens the circuit → interrupts current flow.
5. Fault cleared; fuse must be **manually replaced**.

The melting time is governed by:

$$t_m = \frac{k \cdot A^2}{I^2}$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $I$ is the fault current, and $k$ is a constant depending on material and ambient conditions.

### 2.2 Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB) — Trip Mechanism

An MCB uses a **dual trip mechanism**:

- **Thermal trip (bimetallic strip):** Handles sustained overloads (slow trip). The bimetallic strip bends due to differential thermal expansion → releases the latch.
- **Magnetic trip (solenoid plunger):** Handles short circuits (instantaneous trip). High fault current magnetizes a solenoid → pulls a plunger → trips the latch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two mechanisms?** Because overloads are slow-developing (thermal inertia is acceptable), but short circuits demand interruption within **< 5 ms** to prevent arc damage.

### 2.3 Residual Current Device (RCD / RCCB) — Working Principle

An RCD monitors the **vector sum of currents** in the live (L) and neutral (N) conductors using a **current transformer (CT)**. Under normal conditions:

$$I_L + I_N = 0 \quad \text{(Kirchhoff's Current Law)}$$

The CT secondary produces **zero net flux**. When a leakage current $I_\Delta$ flows to earth (e.g., through a human body), the balance is disturbed:

$$I_L + I_N = -I_\Delta \neq 0$$

The CT secondary now produces a proportional voltage that triggers a **trip coil** within **20–40 ms**, disconnecting the supply. The standard trip sensitivity is **30 mA** for personnel protection and **6 mA** for medical patient protection.

### 2.4 Ground Fault Circuit Interrupter (GFCI)

The GFCI is the **UL-listed (Underwriters Laboratories)** North American counterpart of the RCD, operating on the same differential current principle. It is mandatory in:
- Hospital bathrooms, kitchens
- Wet locations
- Patient care areas within **1.83 m (6 ft)** of a sink

The threshold is fixed at **4–6 mA trip** with a maximum response time of **25 ms**.

### 2.5 Line Isolation Monitor (LIM) — Isolated Power Systems

In **Cardiac Catheterization Labs, ICUs, and Operating Theatres**, the supply is provided through an **Isolation Transformer** (1:1 ratio, typically **5 kVA–10 kVA**) that **isolates the patient from earth**. An LIM continuously monitors the **Total Hazard Current (THC)** and **Line-to-Ground impedance**.

The LIM displays the **Total Hazard Current** in mA and sounds an alarm if it exceeds:

$$\text{THC}_{\max} = 2 \text{ mA (for general medical)} \quad \text{or} \quad 0.5 \text{ mA (for cardiac areas)}$$

### 2.6 Surge Protective Device (SPD)

SPDs protect against **transient overvoltages** (lightning, switching surges) using **Metal Oxide Varistors (MOVs)**, **Gas Discharge Tubes (GDTs)**, or **Transient Voltage Suppression (TVS) diodes**. They clamp the voltage to a safe level:

$$V_{\text{clamp}} \leq V_{\text{rated}} + \Delta V$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Description / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Fuse melting time | $t_m = kA^2 / I^2$ | $t_m$ in seconds, $A$ in m², $I$ in A |
| I²t let-through | $I^2 t$ | Energy passed before trip, in A²·s |
| MCB Trip Curve B | $3 I_n \le I_{trip} \le 5 I_n$ | Resistive loads (lighting) |
| MCB Trip Curve C | $5 I_n \le I_{trip} \le 10 I_n$ | General sockets, small motors |
| MCB Trip Curve D | $10 I_n \le I_{trip} \le 20 I_n$ | Motors, transformers, X-ray |
| Let-through energy limit | $I^2 t \le I^2 t_{\text{characteristic}}$ | Coordination with cable |
| RCD trip current $I_{\Delta n}$ | 30 mA (personnel), 6 mA (patient) | Differential current threshold |
| LIM Hazard Current | THC $\le$ 2 mA (general), 0.5 mA (cardiac) | Monitored continuously |
| Breaking capacity $I_{cn}$ | 6 kA, 10 kA, 16 kA, 25 kA | Maximum interruptible fault |
| Microshock threshold | $I_{\text{hazard}} \ge 10 \ \mu\text{A}$ | Applied directly to myocardium |
| Macroshock threshold | $I_{\text{perception}} \approx 1 \text{ mA}$ | Let-go threshold $\approx 10 \text{ mA}$ |
| Ohm's law (leakage) | $I = V / Z_{\text{body}}$ | $Z_{\text{body}} \approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ wet, $100 \text{ k}\Omega$ dry |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **Multi-Parameter Patient Monitor** connected to mains, a fault in the power supply could induce **50 Hz leakage currents** that superimpose on low-amplitude ECG signals (typically 0.5–5 mV). Without proper circuit protection, a **5 µA leakage** can produce **artefacts** that mimic ventricular fibrillation, leading to false alarms or — worse — masking true arrhythmias. Hence, **medical-grade MCBs, RCDs, and isolation transformers** are non-negotiable in patient-connected equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fuse Pre-Arcing Time (Preece's Law Modified)

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between fusing current and conductor dimensions.

**Step 1 — Heat generated in conductor:**
The power dissipated as heat in a conductor of resistance $R$ carrying current $I$ for time $t$ is:

$$Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 R \, t$$

**Step 2 — Resistance of cylindrical conductor:**
For a conductor of length $l$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$:

$$R = \frac{\rho \, l}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Step 1:**

$$Q_{\text{gen}} = I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho \, l}{A} \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Heat required to raise temperature from $T_0$ to melting point $T_m$:**

$$Q_{\text{req}} = m \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0) = (\rho_m \cdot A \cdot l) \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\rho_m$ is mass density and $c$ is specific heat capacity.

**Step 5 — Equate generated and required heat for threshold (melting) condition:**

$$I^2 \cdot \frac{\rho \, l}{A} \cdot t = \rho_m \cdot A \cdot l \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 6 — Cancel common terms and solve for $t$:**

$$t = \frac{\rho_m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T \cdot A^2}{\rho \cdot I^2}$$

**Step 7 — Define constant $k$:**

$$t_m = \frac{k \, A^2}{I^2} \quad \text{where} \quad k = \frac{\rho_m \, c \, \Delta T}{\rho}$$

> This is **Preece's Equation**, which shows that melting time is **inversely proportional to the square of the fault current** and **directly proportional to the square of the cross-sectional area**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — MCB Trip Time

**Problem:** A 16 A Type-C MCB feeds a circuit. A fault current of 160 A flows. Will the MCB trip, and within what time?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the multiple of rated current:**

$$I_{\text{multiple}} = \frac{160}{16} = 10 \, I_n$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Type-C trip band** ($5 I_n$ to $10 I_n$):

$$I_{\text{multiple}} = 10 \, I_n \in [5 I_n, \, 10 I_n]$$

The MCB **trips magnetically** (instantaneous).

**Step 3 — Manufacturer's typical trip time for Type-C at 10 $I_n$:**

$$t_{\text{trip}} \approx 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The MCB will trip in **~10 ms**, well within the IEC 60898 limit of **0.1 s** for instantaneous magnetic trips.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — RCD Sensitivity Check

**Problem:** A patient in an ICU is connected to a ventilator. The earth leakage measured is **5 mA**. Will a standard 30 mA RCD trip?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compare leakage with RCD trip threshold:**

$$I_{\text{leak}} = 5 \text{ mA} \quad < \quad I_{\Delta n} = 30 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — The RCD will NOT trip.**

**Step 3 — Clinical concern:** Although the RCD does not trip, 5 mA exceeds the **microshock threshold of 10 µA** by 500× if the current reaches the heart (e.g., via a central venous catheter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 30 mA RCD is **inadequate for cardiac-protected areas**. The correct solution is an **isolated power system (IPS)** with a **5 mA–10 mA LIM trip threshold** combined with equipotential bonding, as per **IEC 60364-7-710**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protector Selection

| Protector | Operating Principle | Trip Time | Typical Rating | Clinical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fuse (HRC) | Melting of alloy element | 0.1 s – few s | 0.5 A – 1250 A | Equipment primary protection |
| MCB Type-B | Thermal + magnetic | 0.01 – 30 s | 6 A – 125 A | Lighting, sockets (general) |
| MCB Type-C | Thermal + magnetic | 0.01 – 30 s | 6 A – 125 A | Small motors, general wards |
| MCB Type-D | Thermal + magnetic | 0.01 – 30 s | 6 A – 125 A | X-ray, CT, MRI, large motors |
| RCCB / RCD | Differential CT | 20 – 40 ms | 25 A – 63 A, 30 mA / 300 mA | Wet areas, patient bathrooms |
| GFCI | Differential CT | < 25 ms | 15 A – 20 A, 4–6 mA | North American patient areas |
| LIM | Continuous monitor + alarm | 1 – 5 s alarm delay | 5–10 kVA IT system | OT, ICU, Cath lab |
| SPD / MOV | Voltage clamping | < 25 ns | 10 kA – 100 kA | Main panel, equipment input |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Hospital Power Distribution & Protection Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mains[HT Mains Supply 11 kV] --> TR[Step Down Transformer 11kV to 415V]
    TR --> SPD1[Surge Protective Device SPD Class 1]
    SPD1 --> MCCB1[Molded Case Circuit Breaker MCCB 630A]
    MCCB1 --> Bus[Main LT Bus Bar 415V Three Phase]
    Bus --> MCBGen[MCB Type C 63A General Wards]
    Bus --> MCBICU[MCB Type D 40A ICU Power]
    Bus --> MCBOT[MCB Type D 32A Operating Theatre]
    Bus --> MCBXray[MCB Type D 100A Radiology X Ray]
    MCBGen --> RCDGen[RCD 30mA Personnel Protection]
    MCBICU --> Iso1[Isolation Transformer 5kVA 1 to 1]
    Iso1 --> LIM1[Line Isolation Monitor LIM 2mA Alarm]
    LIM1 --> ICUOutlet[ICU Patient Bedside Outlet Group 1]
    MCBOT --> Iso2[Isolation Transformer 10kVA]
    Iso2 --> LIM2[Line Isolation Monitor LIM 0.5mA Alarm]
    LIM2 --> OTOutlet[OT Receptacles and Equipment Sockets]
    MCBXray --> Xray[X Ray Machine High Tension Input]
    RCDGen --> GenOutlet[General Ward Outlets and Lighting]
    TR --> Earth[Hospital Equipotential Earth Bus Impedance less than 0.3 Ohm]
    Iso1 --> Earth
    Iso2 --> Earth
    MCBGen --> Earth
    MCBICU --> Earth
    MCBOT --> Earth
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — RCD Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LSupply[Live Conductor L] --> CT1[Current Transformer Primary Winding L turn]
    NSupply[Neutral Conductor N] --> CT2[Current Transformer Primary Winding N turn]
    CT1 --> CT3[Toroidal Ferrite Core]
    CT2 --> CT3
    CT3 --> Sec[Secondary Winding]
    Sec --> Amp[Sense Amplifier and Comparator]
    Amp --> Trip[Trip Coil Solenoid]
    Trip --> CB[Main Contacts Open]
    Leakage[Leakage Path to Earth through human body or fault] --> CT1
    Leakage --> CT2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In a healthy circuit, the currents in L and N windings are equal and opposite, so the net flux in the toroid is **zero** and the secondary sees no signal. When leakage flows to earth, the L and N currents become unequal, the toroidal core develops a **net flux**, and the induced EMF in the secondary drives the trip coil.

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Protection Device Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Load to be Protected] --> Q1{Is it a cardiac protected area?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> IPS[Use Isolated Power System with LIM and 5 to 10 mA trip]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it in a wet or patient bathroom area?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> RCD30[Use 30 mA RCD with MCB Type B upstream]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Inrush current high? Motors, X ray, MRI}
    Q3 -- Yes --> MCBD[MCB Type D with appropriate breaking capacity]
    Q3 -- No --> MCBC[MCB Type C with 30 mA RCD]
    IPS --> SPDCheck{Transient overvoltage risk?}
    RCDCheck --> SPDCheck
    MCBD --> SPDCheck
    MCBC --> SPDCheck
    SPDCheck -- Yes --> SPDadd[Add SPD at distribution board]
    SPDCheck -- No --> End[Protective scheme finalized]
    SPDadd --> End
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define a **fuse** and explain the **Preece equation** in the context of its operation.

**Model Answer:**

A fuse is a one-time protective device containing a calibrated metallic element that melts and opens the circuit when the current exceeds a predetermined value for a sufficient duration. It is governed by **Preece's equation**:

$$t_m = \frac{k \cdot A^2}{I^2}$$

where $t_m$ is the pre-arcing (melting) time, $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the fuse element, $I$ is the fault current, and $k$ is a constant depending on the material's specific heat, density, resistivity, and the temperature rise required. The equation shows that **melting time decreases with the square of fault current** — a 10× increase in fault current reduces melting time by a factor of 100.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **MCB Type B, Type C, and Type D** in terms of their trip characteristics and applications.

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Instantaneous Trip Band | Typical Loads | Application in Hospital |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type B** | $3 I_n$ to $5 I_n$ | Resistive (heaters, lighting) | Hospital corridor lighting, office loads |
| **Type C** | $5 I_n$ to $10 I_n$ | Moderate inductive (small motors) | General ward sockets, bedside outlets |
| **Type D** | $10 I_n$ to $20 I_n$ | High inductive (large motors, transformers) | X-ray machines, CT scanners, MRI, OT pendants |

Type B has the **highest sensitivity** (trips on small overloads) while Type D tolerates the **highest inrush** without nuisance tripping.

---

### Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **working principle of a Residual Current Device (RCD)**. How does it differ from a standard MCB? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An RCD operates on the principle of **Kirchhoff's Current Law** applied through a **toroidal current transformer**.

1. **Live (L) and Neutral (N)** conductors pass through a **ferrite toroidal core** as primary windings (each of 1 turn).
2. Under normal conditions, $I_L = I_N$ and they flow in **opposite directions**, so the net magnetic flux in the core is **zero**. The secondary winding produces **no induced EMF**.
3. When a **leakage current** $I_\Delta$ flows to earth (through a human body or insulation fault), $I_L \neq I_N$ and the imbalance equals $I_\Delta$.
4. This imbalance produces a **net alternating flux** in the toroid, inducing an EMF in the secondary.
5. The sense amplifier detects this EMF, compares it with a reference, and energizes a **trip solenoid** that opens the main contacts within **20–40 ms**.

**Difference from MCB:**

| Feature | MCB | RCD |
|---|---|---|
| Detects | Overcurrent / short circuit | Leakage to earth (differential current) |
| Senses | Magnitude of current | Difference between L and N currents |
| Sensitivity | A few amperes | As low as **6 mA – 30 mA** |
| Protects against | Equipment damage, fire | **Electric shock** to humans |

---

**(b)** A **Single-Phase 230 V, 50 Hz** hospital ward circuit is protected by a **30 mA RCD** and a **16 A Type-C MCB**. Calculate:
- (i) The **minimum earth-loop impedance** required for the MCB to trip on a bolted short circuit. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) Comment on whether the RCD will provide additional shock protection. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Minimum earth-loop impedance:**

For a Type-C MCB, the magnetic trip occurs at $10 \times I_n = 10 \times 16 = 160$ A (instantaneous region).

By Ohm's Law, the minimum loop impedance to drive this current is:

$$Z_{\text{loop,min}} = \frac{V}{I_{\text{trip}}} = \frac{230}{160} = 1.4375 \ \Omega$$

Therefore, $Z_{\text{loop}} \le 1.44 \ \Omega$ is required.

**(ii) Role of RCD:**

Yes, the RCD provides **critical supplementary protection** because:
- The MCB only trips on **high currents (≥ 80 A for thermal, ≥ 160 A for magnetic)** — it cannot detect low-level leakage currents of **30 mA** that are lethal to humans.
- A **30 mA RCD trips within 30 ms**, preventing ventricular fibrillation (which requires sustained currents of 30 mA for > 30 ms).
- Together, **MCB + RCD = combined overcurrent and personnel protection**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the need for an **Isolated Power System (IPS)** in **cardiac catheterization laboratories**. Describe the role of the **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)** with reference to **IEC 60364-7-710**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In cardiac cath labs, patients may have **intracardiac catheters** that provide a **direct conductive path to the myocardium**. Currents as low as **10 µA** at 50/60 Hz can induce **ventricular fibrillation (microshock)**.

A standard TN (Terra-Neutral) grounded system cannot guarantee safety because any **first fault** would already drive dangerous current through the patient. Hence, an **Isolated Power System (IT system)** is mandated.

**Components:**
1. **Medical Isolation Transformer** — 1:1 ratio, low leakage capacitance, typically **3.5 kVA – 10 kVA**, with reinforced insulation.
2. **Line Isolation Monitor (LIM)** — continuously measures the **impedance from each line to ground** and the **Total Hazard Current (THC)**.

**LIM Operation:**
- Measures the **line-to-ground impedance** ($Z_1$, $Z_2$) via a low-frequency test signal.
- Computes THC = $V \cdot (1/Z_1 + 1/Z_2)$.
- **Alarm thresholds (per IEC 60364-7-710):**
   - First fault: alarm only, **no interruption** of supply.
   - THC > **2 mA** (general medical) or **0.5 mA** (cardiac): audible + visual alarm.
- LIM ensures that **surgery is not interrupted** by a single fault — critical during open-heart procedures.

---

**(b)** A **500 VA, 230 V** isolation transformer has a primary-to-secondary leakage capacitance of **200 pF**. Calculate the **leakage current** flowing through a person with body resistance **2 kΩ** who touches the secondary live conductor while grounded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Capacitive reactance of leakage path at 50 Hz:**

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 200 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$X_C = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.5915 \times 10^{7} \ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Impedance of the series combination (capacitor in series with body):**

$$Z_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{R_{\text{body}}^2 + X_C^2} \approx X_C \quad \text{(since } X_C \gg R_{\text{body}} \text{)}$$

$$Z_{\text{total}} \approx 1.5915 \times 10^{7} \ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Leakage current through the body:**

$$I_{\text{leak}} = \frac{V}{Z_{\text{total}}} = \frac{230}{1.5915 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$I_{\text{leak}} = 1.445 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} = 14.45 \ \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Safety assessment:**

$$I_{\text{leak}} = 14.45 \ \mu\text{A} > 10 \ \mu\text{A} \ \text{(microshock threshold)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** This leakage current **exceeds the microshock threshold**. The isolation transformer's **leakage capacitance must be minimized** (using shielded windings and special insulation) to keep $I_{\text{leak}} < 10 \ \mu\text{A}$ in cardiac-protected areas. Practically, medical isolation transformers specify leakage current **< 50 µA** under single-fault conditions.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RCD with MCB:** RCDs detect **differential** current (leakage); MCBs detect **overcurrent**. Writing "RCD trips on overcurrent" is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Wrong threshold values:** Writing "RCD trips at 30 mA" without specifying **30 mA = personnel protection** and **6 mA = medical/patient protection** loses a mark.
> 3. **Mixing TN and IT systems:** In cardiac areas, students often forget that an IT system **does not trip on first fault** — it only **alarms**. This is the entire purpose of LIM.
> 4. **Microshock vs Macroshock thresholds:** Confusing **10 µA (microshock)** with **10 mA (let-go threshold for macroshock)** is a **3-mark killer**.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Writing $I = V/R$ without converting kΩ correctly. Always show units step-by-step.
> 6. **Skipping the line before/after LaTeX:** KTU digital evaluation systems penalize malformed LaTeX rendering.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **fuse** is a **one-time** overcurrent protector governed by **Preece's equation** $t_m = kA^2/I^2$.
- ✅ An **MCB** combines a **thermal trip** (overload) and a **magnetic trip** (short circuit) — resettable.
- ✅ **MCB Trip Curves:** **B** (3–5 In) → lighting; **C** (5–10 In) → sockets; **D** (10–20 In) → motors, X-ray, MRI.
- ✅ An **RCD/RCCB** detects **differential current** between L and N using a **toroidal CT**; trip thresholds: **30 mA (personnel)**, **6 mA (medical)**.
- ✅ A **GFCI** is the **North American** equivalent of an RCD (trip at 4–6 mA in < 25 ms).
- ✅ **Isolated Power Systems (IPS)** are **mandatory** in cardiac cath labs, OT, and ICU per **IEC 60364-7-710**.
- ✅ The **LIM** monitors **Total Hazard Current (THC)** and alarms at **2 mA (general)** or **0.5 mA (cardiac)**.
- ✅ **Macroshock** threshold: **1 mA perception, 10 mA let-go, 30–50 mA respiratory paralysis, 100 mA ventricular fibrillation** (skin contact).
- ✅ **Microshock** threshold: **10 µA** directly to the myocardium via catheter.
- ✅ **SPDs** use **MOVs/GDTs** to clamp transient overvoltages within **< 25 ns**.
- ✅ Hospital earth resistance must be **< 0.3 Ω** for equipotential bonding; TN-C-S (PME) is generally **prohibited** in patient areas.
- ✅ **Breaking capacity** of MCB must exceed the **prospective short-circuit current** at the point of installation.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers; KTU examiners **specifically check for unit consistency**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surge Protectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT413 - CLINICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surge Protectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Surge Protector** (also termed a *Transient Voltage Surge Suppressor* — TVSS, or *Surge Protective Device* — SPD per IEC 61643-1) is a protective apparatus installed in an electrical power distribution network, specifically engineered to limit transient overvoltages by diverting or suppressing surge currents, thereby safeguarding downstream sensitive biomedical instrumentation from voltage spikes, switching transients, and induced lightning disturbances.

In the context of **Clinical Engineering**, surge protectors form a critical element of the *Medical Electrical Equipment Safety Chain* (per **IEC 60601-1**) by ensuring that electromagnetic disturbances (EMD) and conducted transients do not compromise the basic safety and essential performance of life-supporting and life-sustaining medical devices such as ventilators, defibrillators, patient monitors, and anesthesia workstations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Clinical Engineering Module 2 mandates the study of surge protectors as a *passive safety infrastructure component* under the broader umbrella of hospital electrical safety. The examiner expects definitions, classification, working principles, and selection criteria.

### 1.2 Core Terminology (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

- **Surge:** A short-duration (microsecond to millisecond) overvoltage event superimposed on the nominal AC waveform, typically with peak amplitude exceeding **110% of nominal RMS voltage**.
- **Clamping Voltage ($V_c$):** The maximum voltage the surge protector permits to pass through to the load before clamping action begins.
